text: I naturally step into the lead when a group seems directionless.
I prefer to let someone else set the direction before I contribute.
I believe that someone needs to take charge, and it might as well be me.
I find that groups work best when no single person dominates the conversation.
I often find myself telling others what needs to happen next.
I hold the view that the best outcomes come from shared decision-making.
I feel most alive when I am steering the course of a project.
I am comfortable letting others make the final call.
When I see an opportunity, I move quickly to claim it.
I wait to hear everyone's perspective before forming a group plan.
I tend to assert my position firmly in discussions.
I usually adjust my stance to keep the group aligned.
I have a strong instinct to organize people toward a goal.
I find satisfaction in helping someone else's vision come to life.
I feel that hesitation costs more than imperfect action.
I believe that patience and collective input lead to wiser choices.
I am the person who usually calls the meeting or sets the agenda.
I prefer when someone else organizes the agenda and I can support their plan.
I enjoy being the one others look to for decisions.
I am happiest when I can contribute without being the focal point.
I often initiate projects without waiting for approval.
I like to wait until a clear consensus emerges before acting.
I find it energizing to direct a team through a challenging situation.
I find it energizing to be part of a team that navigates challenges together.
I believe clarity of vision matters more than everyone's agreement.
I believe that agreement among the group matters more than speed.
When there is a vacuum of leadership, I fill it without thinking twice.
When there is a vacuum of leadership, I look around to see who else might step in.
I hold the view that decisive action is a form of respect for everyone's time.
I hold the view that soliciting everyone's input is a form of respect.
I feel confident making decisions on behalf of a group.
I feel uneasy making decisions that affect others without their explicit buy-in.
I have been told that I have a commanding presence.
I have been told that I am a great team player.
I tend to set the pace in group work rather than follow it.
I tend to match the pace others have set rather than impose my own.
I find that my ideas often shape the direction a group takes.
I find that I often refine and build on ideas others have introduced.
I prefer to give clear directives rather than open-ended suggestions.
I prefer to offer suggestions rather than give directives.
I feel that if I don't take the reins, things won't move forward.
I trust that the group will find its way forward without one person driving.
I am drawn to roles where I can shape outcomes directly.
I am drawn to roles where I support and enable others' success.
I frequently volunteer to be the spokesperson or point person.
I am happy to let someone else be the spokesperson.
I believe that strong leadership requires the willingness to override dissent when needed.
I believe that strong leadership means facilitating agreement, not overriding it.
I feel restless when no one is taking charge of a situation.
I feel comfortable in ambiguous situations where no one has taken charge yet.
I make decisions quickly and stand by them.
I like to take time and let the group deliberate before a decision is made.
I enjoy the weight of responsibility that comes with leading.
I prefer shared responsibility to personal accountability for outcomes.
I instinctively delegate tasks and coordinate efforts.
I prefer to receive tasks from a coordinator rather than assign them.
I find myself correcting course when I think a group is going the wrong way.
I let the group find its own course, even if I see a different path.
I often set the vision and expect others to execute it.
I prefer to help execute a shared vision rather than set one alone.
I feel that a clear chain of command produces better results.
I feel that flat, collaborative structures produce better results.
I naturally claim authority in informal group settings.
I naturally defer to others in informal group settings.
I would rather make a bold, imperfect decision than let the group stall.
I would rather slow down and ensure everyone is on board.
I take pride in being the driving force behind a project's success.
I take pride in the behind-the-scenes work that makes a project succeed.
I feel energized when people follow my lead.
I feel energized when a group reaches a genuine consensus.
I often push for my preferred approach in group decisions.
I often yield to the group's preferred approach, even if mine differs.
I believe I have a talent for seeing what needs to be done and making it happen.
I believe I have a talent for bringing people together around a shared purpose.
I regularly take initiative in situations where others hesitate.
I regularly step back so others have space to take initiative.
I am most productive when I have full autonomy over direction.
I am most productive when direction has been established collectively.
I find it hard to stay quiet when I disagree with the group's direction.
I find it easy to go along with the group's direction, even when I have reservations.
I see leadership as a core part of my identity.
I see collaboration as a core part of my identity.
I am comfortable overruling a group decision when I believe I am right.
I rarely overrule a group decision, even when I think there is a better option.
I often end up as the de facto leader in group projects.
I often end up as the reliable supporter in group projects.
I feel that it is my responsibility to point the way when people are lost.
I feel that the group should find its own way rather than rely on one person.
I prefer to own the outcome of a decision rather than share that ownership.
I prefer joint ownership of outcomes so that no single person bears the weight.
I am comfortable telling people directly what I need from them.
I prefer to frame requests as invitations rather than directives.
I hold the view that groups need a strong voice to stay focused.
I hold the view that groups find focus naturally when everyone's voice is heard.
I enjoy crafting the strategy and assigning roles.
I enjoy being assigned a role and doing it well.
I feel drawn to positions of formal authority.
I feel more comfortable in positions without formal authority.
When I have a vision, I pursue it whether or not others initially agree.
When I have a vision, I wait to see if others share it before moving forward.
I believe indecision is more dangerous than a wrong decision.
I believe a premature decision is more dangerous than a delayed one.
I am the one who usually proposes the plan for a group activity.
I usually wait for someone else to propose a plan before weighing in.
I feel that my confidence in decisions helps reassure the people around me.
I feel that gathering broad input helps reassure the people around me.
I naturally speak first in group discussions.
I naturally listen first and speak after others have shared their thoughts.
I often take it upon myself to resolve group disagreements by choosing a path.
I often help resolve group disagreements by finding common ground.
I have a strong desire to leave my mark on the things I work on.
I am content to contribute to something larger without needing personal recognition.
I feel most at ease when I am the one setting expectations.
I feel most at ease when expectations are set collaboratively.
I trust my own judgment more than the group's consensus.
I trust the group's consensus more than my own individual judgment.
I frequently take on captain or leadership roles in team settings.
I frequently take on supporting or facilitating roles in team settings.
I feel compelled to act when I see inefficiency or lack of direction.
I feel that groups benefit from working through inefficiency at their own pace.
I am comfortable making unpopular decisions if I believe they are correct.
I prefer to find a decision everyone can accept, even if it means compromise.
I find it natural to give instructions and coordinate others.
I find it natural to follow well-given instructions and coordinate with peers.
I think of myself as someone who drives outcomes.
I think of myself as someone who facilitates outcomes.
I often establish the norms and expectations within a group.
I often adapt to the norms and expectations already present in a group.
I feel frustrated when leadership is unclear or absent.
I feel comfortable operating without a clearly designated leader.
I find that others naturally look to me for direction.
I find that others naturally see me as a reliable collaborator.
I want to be the one who shapes how a project unfolds.
I want to be part of shaping how a project unfolds, alongside others.
I believe that decisiveness is one of my core strengths.
I believe that adaptability is one of my core strengths.
I would rather lead a small group than follow in a large one.
I would rather be an integral part of a large group than lead a small one.
I feel that my directness helps groups move forward.
I feel that my willingness to accommodate helps groups stay together.
I am quick to take ownership of a situation when things go sideways.
When things go sideways, I look for a collective response rather than taking personal charge.
I am at my best when I am in the driver's seat.
I am at my best when I am a trusted co-pilot.
I hold the view that someone must ultimately be accountable, and I accept that role.
I hold the view that accountability should be distributed across the team.
I notice that I frequently steer conversations toward action.
I notice that I frequently steer conversations toward inclusion.
I enjoy the challenge of persuading others to follow my direction.
I prefer to build agreement organically rather than persuade.
I often set deadlines and hold people to them.
I prefer flexible timelines that the group agrees upon together.
I think it is important to make a stand and defend it.
I think it is important to remain open and willing to shift with the group.
I am energized by having the final say.
I am energized by arriving at a decision that everyone shaped.
I find that I can mobilize people toward a goal effectively.
I find that I can help people feel heard and valued within a group.
I would describe my influence style as directive.
I would describe my influence style as collaborative.
I often establish priorities for the group without being asked.
I prefer that priorities be established through group discussion.
I feel a strong pull to be in charge of important initiatives.
I feel a strong pull to be deeply involved in important initiatives, though not necessarily in charge.
I tend to assert boundaries for the group about what is and isn't acceptable.
I tend to let the group define its own boundaries collectively.
I believe that my willingness to decide quickly is valuable to those around me.
I believe that my willingness to defer is valuable to those around me.
I am comfortable standing alone in my position if I think it is right.
I find it important to stay aligned with the group, even at the cost of my personal preference.
I see myself as someone who makes things happen.
I see myself as someone who helps things happen.
I frequently redefine objectives when I think the current ones are wrong.
I rarely redefine objectives that were set by the group.
I take the lead in negotiations and high-stakes conversations.
I prefer a supportive role in negotiations and high-stakes conversations.
I feel responsible for keeping a group on track toward its goals.
I feel responsible for keeping a group connected and cohesive.
I am energized by the act of making a consequential choice.
I am energized by the process of building a decision together.
I generally dictate the terms of a collaboration.
I generally negotiate the terms of a collaboration with all parties.
I feel that having a single decision-maker leads to clearer outcomes.
I feel that collaborative decision-making leads to more durable outcomes.
I am someone who defines the mission rather than inheriting it.
I am someone who embraces and strengthens a shared mission.
I feel that taking charge is a natural extension of caring about results.
I feel that yielding space to others is a natural extension of caring about the group.
I often present my conclusions as settled rather than open for debate.
I often present my thoughts as starting points for group discussion.
I am attracted to opportunities where I can set the tone for a team.
I am attracted to opportunities where I can blend into a well-functioning team.
I find it difficult to sit back when I have a clear idea of what should be done.
I find it natural to sit back and let the group work through its process.
I often champion a particular direction and rally others behind it.
I often help synthesize multiple perspectives into a direction the group can support.
I believe that strong personal conviction is necessary for effective leadership.
I believe that openness to others' views is necessary for effective teamwork.
I tend to announce my decisions rather than propose them for feedback.
I tend to propose ideas and invite feedback before moving forward.
I am the type of person who builds the roadmap.
I am the type of person who walks the roadmap alongside others.
I have a bias toward action over deliberation.
I have a bias toward deliberation over premature action.
I enjoy being the public face of a group effort.
I enjoy doing meaningful work that does not require being the public face.
I find that I set the emotional and strategic tone in groups I join.
I find that I adapt to the tone already present in groups I join.
I have a hard time following when I think I could lead better.
I am comfortable following even when I might have a different approach.
I am willing to shoulder the burden of an unpopular choice.
I prefer to find a path everyone can accept rather than force a choice.
I tend to frame requests as expectations rather than options.
I tend to frame requests as options rather than expectations.
I feel that my job is to point the way, even if others resist.
I feel that my job is to support the group in finding its own way.
I am quick to take the microphone, literally or figuratively.
I am happy to pass the microphone to whoever wants it.
I often initiate change rather than wait for conditions to force it.
I often wait for the group to signal readiness before pursuing change.
I am the person who turns a vague idea into a concrete plan of action.
I am the person who helps refine and improve someone else's plan of action.
I feel that if no one leads, nothing meaningful gets accomplished.
I feel that meaningful things can be accomplished without anyone formally leading.
I instinctively take control of high-pressure situations.
In high-pressure situations, I look to collaborate and distribute the load.
I prefer relationships where I set the agenda.
I prefer relationships where the agenda emerges mutually.
I feel empowered when I can make executive decisions.
I feel empowered when the group trusts me enough to share power.
I often find that I am the one defining what success looks like.
I often find that I help the group define what success looks like together.
I am not afraid to overrule others when the stakes are high.
I am cautious about overruling others, even when the stakes are high.
I feel that waiting for full consensus wastes valuable momentum.
I feel that building consensus creates stronger momentum in the long run.
I tend to carve out my own lane rather than share one.
I tend to find ways to collaborate within a shared lane.
I am the person others call when something needs to get decided.
I am the person others call when something needs broad buy-in.
I believe that a leader who hesitates loses credibility.
I believe that a leader who does not listen loses the group's trust.
I prefer to be the architect of a project rather than a contributor.
I prefer to be a contributor alongside others rather than the sole architect.
I instinctively create structure where there is none.
I instinctively adapt to whatever structure the group has established.
I find it satisfying to override inertia and get things moving.
I find it satisfying to maintain cohesion as the group moves forward.
I think of authority as something to be exercised, not just held.
I think of authority as something to be shared, not concentrated.
I regularly push back when I disagree with the prevailing direction.
I regularly accommodate the prevailing direction to maintain group flow.
I feel that being in control of a situation allows me to do my best work.
I feel that being in a supportive role allows me to do my best work.
I prefer to ask for forgiveness rather than permission.
I prefer to get buy-in before acting on behalf of the group.
I am known for taking decisive stands in group deliberations.
I am known for bridging differences in group deliberations.
I believe a project needs one clear leader to succeed.
I believe a project succeeds best with distributed leadership.
I feel that my assertiveness is one of my greatest assets.
I feel that my flexibility is one of my greatest assets.
I am inclined to make a choice and commit rather than keep options open.
I am inclined to keep options open until the group has weighed in.
I often position myself as the final decision-maker in group settings.
I often position myself as a facilitator in group settings.
I am drawn to situations where my voice carries significant weight.
I am drawn to situations where every voice carries equal weight.
I tend to direct the flow of conversation toward outcomes I envision.
I tend to let conversations flow where the group takes them.
I find it natural to tell others what I expect from them.
I find it natural to ask others what they expect from themselves.
I view taking charge as a service to the group.
I view deferring to others as a service to the group.
I feel most confident when I am directing a process from start to finish.
I feel most confident when I am contributing to a process others are co-directing.
I would rather be the founder than the early employee.
I would rather be a key early contributor than the founder.
I prefer to pitch my idea and get others on board with it.
I prefer to collect others' ideas and find the common thread.
I am the type who creates the rules of engagement for a group.
I am the type who helps the group discover its own rules of engagement.
I thrive when I have the authority to make things happen.
I thrive when I have the trust of a group that works together.
I feel that a well-directed team outperforms a democratic one.
I feel that a democratic team outperforms a top-down one.
I often take the initiative to resolve ambiguity for others.
I often help the group sit with ambiguity until clarity emerges together.
I feel responsible for making sure the right things happen.
I feel responsible for making sure everyone is part of what happens.
I am comfortable exercising authority even when it creates tension.
I am inclined to avoid exercising authority if it would create tension.
I see myself as the captain of whatever ship I am on.
I see myself as a valued crew member of whatever ship I am on.
I gravitate toward being the one who decides when a discussion has gone on long enough.
I gravitate toward letting discussions continue until the group is satisfied.
I frequently introduce new initiatives without waiting for a mandate.
I prefer that new initiatives come from a collective mandate.
I find that I am usually the one setting the standards for a group.
I find that I am usually the one helping the group uphold its own standards.
